    Fractal Structure of Star Clusters

    Fractal Structure of Star Clusters

    Type:Intermediate concept
    Field:Star cluster formation, stellar dynamics
    Key metric:Q parameter
    Related: Star formation
    Molecular cloud
    Star cluster formation
    Dynamical relaxation
    Subcluster formation

    Fractal structure in young star clusters refers to the self-similar, hierarchically clumped spatial distribution of stars that characterizes newly formed stellar populations. Rather than being smoothly distributed, young clusters show subgroups within subgroups — a pattern inherited from the turbulent, fractal density field of their parent molecular cloud.12

    Origin: Inheritance from Molecular Clouds

    Giant molecular clouds are themselves fractal structures, shaped by supersonic turbulence and gravity. When a cloud undergoes gravoturbulent collapse34, it fragments into dense clumps and filaments that serve as nurseries for stellar subclusters.56 The resulting young cluster naturally inherits the spatial hierarchy of its birth environment.

    Observations from the Herschel Space Observatory confirm that filamentary molecular cloud networks give rise to chains of dense cores and proto-clusters78, while X-ray and near-infrared surveys of young stellar clusters (e.g., with Chandra) reveal rich subcluster populations within OB associations and star-forming regions.9

    Dynamical Erasure

    Fractal substructure is not permanent. As clusters evolve dynamically, gravitational interactions smooth out the initial clumpiness — a process called dynamical relaxation. The timescale for fractal erasure has been quantified in multiple simulations:

    • Laverde-Villarreal et al. (2025)10 and Akhmetali et al. (2026)11 both find fractal erasure at approximately 2.5 free-fall times (tff), across cloud masses ranging from ~2500 M☉ to ~3 × 105 M☉.
    • Earlier work by Maschberger et al. (2010)12 found low initial Q values rising to non-fractal values within a couple of free-fall times in gravitationally bound clusters.
    • Ballone et al. (2020)13 found that very massive clusters (104–105 M☉) remained fractal after 2 tff, possibly due to different initial density profiles.

    Role of Stellar Feedback

    Stellar feedback from massive stars can both accelerate and delay fractal erasure. Photoionization and stellar winds can disperse surrounding gas, triggering secondary subcluster formation in compressed shells — prolonging fractal structure beyond 4 tff in some simulations.11 However, this effect is stochastic, depending critically on when and where a massive star forms.11 In other simulations, massive stars form without measurably enhancing fractality.

    Measuring Fractal Structure

    Three main diagnostics are used:

    • Q parameter (Cartwright & Whitworth 20041): ratio of normalized MST edge length to mean interparticle separation. Q < 0.8 (2D) or < 0.7 (3D) indicates fractal structure.
    • Box-counting dimension: slope of ln N(L) vs. −ln L; global measure of spatial extent.14
    • Correlation dimension: slope of the correlation integral C(r) vs. ln r; sensitive to local clustering.1516

    The Q parameter and correlation dimension are more sensitive to local substructure, while box-counting dimension captures global spatial coverage. The correlation dimension shows a moderate positive correlation with Q, while box-counting shows only a weak correlation.11

    Open Questions

    • Does the ~2.5 tff erasure timescale hold for very different initial density profiles (e.g., flat or power-law)?
    • What is the 3D fractal structure of real young clusters, as opposed to projected 2D observations?
    • How does the fractal structure of a cluster at birth affect its long-term survival and dissolution into the field?

    See Also

    • Q parameter (fractality) — the primary diagnostic
    • Star cluster formation
    • Dynamical relaxation in clusters
    • Subcluster formation
    • Stellar feedback
    • Molecular cloud

    References
    1. Cartwright, A. & Whitworth, A. P. (2004). "The statistical analysis of star clusters." MNRAS, 348, 589–598.
    2. Sanchez, N. & Alfaro, E. J. (2009). "The spatial distribution of stars in open clusters." ApJ, 696, 2086.
    3. Larson, R. B. (1981). "Turbulence and star formation in molecular clouds." MNRAS, 194, 809–826.
    4. Mac Low, M.-M. & Klessen, R. S. (2004). "Control of star formation by supersonic turbulence." Rev. Mod. Phys., 76, 125.
    5. Vázquez-Semadeni, E. et al. (2017). "Hierarchical star cluster assembly in globally collapsing molecular clouds." MNRAS, 467, 1313–1328.
    6. Grudić, M. Y. et al. (2018). "From the top down and back up again: star cluster structure from hierarchical star formation." MNRAS, 481, 688–702.
    7. André, Ph. et al. (2010). "From filamentary clouds to prestellar cores to the stellar IMF." A&A, 518, L102.
    8. André, P. et al. (2014). "From filamentary networks to dense cores in molecular clouds." Protostars and Planets VI.
    9. Kuhn, M. A. et al. (2014). "The spatial structure of young stellar clusters. I. Subclusters." ApJ, 787, 107.
    10. Laverde-Villarreal, E. et al. (2025). "The Evolution of Substructure during Star Cluster Assembly." ApJ, 989, 22. doi:10.3847/1538-4357/ade9b5
    11. Akhmetali, A. et al. (2026). "Evolution of fractality in centrally concentrated young clusters." A&A (submitted). arXiv:2603.16183
    12. Maschberger, Th. et al. (2010). "Properties of hierarchically forming star clusters." MNRAS, 404, 1061–1080.
    13. Ballone, A. et al. (2020). "Evolution of fractality and rotation in embedded star clusters." MNRAS, 496, 49–59.
    14. Imre, A. R. & Bogaert, J. (2006). "The Minkowski-Bouligand dimension." Fractals, 14, 49–53.
    15. Grassberger, P. (1983). "On the fractal dimension of the Henon attractor." Physics Letters A, 97, 224–226.
    16. de La Fuente Marcos, R. & de La Fuente Marcos, C. (2006). "The fractal dimensions of the spatial distribution of young open clusters." A&A, 452, 163–168.
